content.text: Model routing ADR patterns are a set of Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) that formally capture how an agentic AI system selects, prioritises, and falls back between large language models at runtime. Rather than hard-coding a single model name throughout the codebase, a routing layer sits between the application and every LLM provider, applying a decision policy — capability check, cost threshold, latency target, or quality signal — to direct each request to the most appropriate model for that exact moment.
An ADR for model routing documents

a particular routing strategy was chosen, what alternatives were rejected, and which constraints (cost budget, latency SLA, regulatory requirements, or capability gaps) drove the decision. This is not operational documentation — it is a durable record of a one-time architectural choice, written at the time the decision was made, so that future engineers can understand the reasoning rather than just the outcome.

Routing decisions are notoriously hard to revisit. A team that spent three weeks benchmarking five models and concluded "use claude-3-5-haiku for summarisation and claude-opus-4 for reasoning" will lose that institutional knowledge as engineers change projects. An ADR preserves it: the benchmarks, the rejected alternatives, the cost assumptions, and the acceptance criteria that would trigger a re-evaluation. When the next generation of models arrives, the ADR makes the upgrade conversation concrete rather than speculative.

Agentic systems regularly handle heterogeneous workloads: a single pipeline might classify a document (cheap, fast, low stakes), extract structured data (moderate complexity), reason over a multi-step plan (expensive, latency-tolerant), and generate a user-facing summary (quality-critical). Using one model for all four tasks either over-spends on simple work or under-serves complex work. A routing layer makes each task match the model that serves it best — but only if the routing policy is explicit and auditable.
Without a routing ADR, the policy lives in someone's head. The first time a junior engineer touches the inference code, they see

hard-coded on every call and assume that is the correct choice. They have no visibility into the deliberate cost optimisation that placed haiku on classification tasks, or the fact that a senior architect tested three alternatives and measured a 4x cost saving. An ADR surfaces that history immediately.

Reliability compounds the importance. LLM APIs experience outages, rate limits, and capacity throttles. A system with no documented fallback chain enters an undocumented emergency state when the primary model becomes unavailable. An ADR for fallback logic defines the chain — primary to secondary to tertiary — and records why that specific ordering was chosen (latency, cost, capability compatibility, licensing). This turns crisis response from improvised to procedural.
Routing cheap tasks to cheap models and expensive tasks to capable-but-pricier models can reduce inference spend by 60–80% with no quality regression on the simple cases.

Time-sensitive requests — real-time chat turns, autocomplete, UI responsiveness — can be routed to faster, smaller models with sub-200 ms response times while async batch tasks use larger models without a tight latency SLA.
A fallback chain ensures requests succeed even during provider outages. Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures by stopping calls to a model that has been failing for the last N seconds.
ADRs document the routing policy so that all engineers share a common understanding of which model handles what, and under what conditions a fallback occurs.
Regulated industries must demonstrate which model processed which data. Routing decisions logged alongside each request create an audit trail that satisfies compliance requirements.
When a new model generation arrives, ADRs make it easy to evaluate whether the decision criteria that drove the original choice still hold, or whether a re-evaluation is warranted.

A router that handles capability classification, cost thresholds, latency budgets, and circuit-breaker state is a non-trivial component. It has its own bugs, its own test surface, and its own operational overhead.

Intelligent routing requires a task classifier. If the classifier mis-categorises a complex reasoning task as a simple summarisation task, the cheaper model will underdeliver, causing silent quality regressions that are harder to detect than explicit errors.

Different models have different system-prompt behaviours, tool-calling schemas, and instruction-following tendencies. A prompt tuned for the primary model may produce degraded output on the fallback model without modification.

ADRs are living documents only if the team treats them that way. A routing ADR that is filed and forgotten provides false confidence. Each major model release or provider change should trigger a review.

Multi-model routing makes debugging harder: a user complaint about output quality requires tracing which model handled the specific request, what routing criteria applied, and whether a fallback occurred.

A routing layer intercepts every LLM request before it reaches a provider. The router inspects the request — its task type, estimated token count, latency budget, and cost envelope — and applies a policy to select the target model. Four primary routing strategies exist:
(does this model support the required tool schema, context length, or modality?),
(what is the cheapest model that meets a minimum quality bar?),
(which model meets the response-time SLA?), and
(which model achieves the highest score on the relevant benchmark for this task class?). In practice, production routers combine all four as a weighted policy.

Fallback chains add redundancy. A primary model is configured as the preferred target. If it returns a non-retriable error (HTTP 503, rate-limit exhaustion, or context-length overflow) the router demotes to the secondary model. If the secondary also fails, the tertiary is tried. Each level of the chain typically represents a lower-cost, lower-capability, more-available model. The fallback event is logged with the failure reason, so that operations teams can track how often the system degrades to lower-quality models.

The circuit breaker pattern wraps each model endpoint. When a model accumulates N consecutive failures within a time window, the circuit opens: the router stops sending requests to that model entirely and routes directly to the fallback for a configured recovery period. After the period expires, the circuit enters the half-open state: one request is allowed through as a probe. If it succeeds, the circuit closes and normal routing resumes. If it fails, the open state is re-entered. This pattern prevents the router from hammering a failing endpoint and wasting tokens on requests that will time out.

attrs.alt: Model routing and fallback architecture: Incoming Request flows to Intelligent Router which uses capability/cost/latency/quality criteria. Circuit Breaker monitors failure state. Router sends to Primary (claude-opus-4), Secondary (claude-3-5-sonnet), or Tertiary (claude-haiku) models. Dashed red arrows show fail-to-fallback paths. All successful responses flow to Response/Observability layer.
attrs.title: Model Routing and Fallback Architecture

content.text: The diagram shows the request flow from top to bottom. An
, which applies its four-dimensional policy. The
(amber, dashed) tracks per-model health and updates the router's available targets. On the happy path, the router directs to the
(green). On primary failure, the dashed red arrow shows fallback to the
(blue). On secondary failure, another red dashed arrow falls back to the
(amber). All three success paths converge at the
layer (purple), which logs latency, cost, and which model actually served the request.

Model routing ADR patterns are warranted when a system has more than one model in scope, or when there is a concrete expectation that it will. For single-model prototypes, a routing ADR is premature overhead. Once a system handles multiple task types with meaningfully different complexity profiles, or once monthly inference costs become visible on a budget report, the routing decision deserves formal documentation.
the team consciously chooses different models for different task types and wants that reasoning preserved beyond a Slack thread.
Write a fallback chain ADR when
the system must maintain availability above a defined SLA and a single-provider outage would breach it.
Write a circuit breaker ADR when
the team has observed or anticipates repeated provider instability and needs a principled, auditable policy for when to stop calling a failing endpoint.
Do not write a routing ADR when
the system makes fewer than a few hundred LLM calls per day and the cost and complexity of a router would exceed the benefit.
Building a production model router involves three layers: the routing policy, the fallback orchestrator, and the circuit breaker state machine. The steps below cover all three.

Enumerate task types. Run a representative sample of production requests through a task classifier (a lightweight LLM call or rule-based heuristic) and identify distinct capability buckets: simple Q&A, structured extraction, multi-step reasoning, long-context summarisation.

Benchmark models per task type. For each bucket, run candidate models on a test set and record quality score (human eval or automated eval), median latency, and per-request cost. Use these numbers in the ADR as the empirical basis for the routing decision.

Define the routing policy. Express the policy as a priority-ordered list of rules: capability constraints first (hard filters), then cost envelope, then latency budget, then quality preference. Write this into the ADR as the decision record.

Implement the router class. Wrap every LLM call behind a router interface that accepts the task type and request parameters and returns a model selection. Log the selection decision with every response.
Add circuit breaker state per model. Track consecutive failure counts and timestamps. Implement the three-state machine: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (skip this model), HALF-OPEN (one probe allowed).
Wire fallback chains. For each model, configure the next model to try on non-retriable failure. Limit retry depth to avoid infinite fallback loops.

Instrument observability. Emit a metric or log line on every routing decision: task type, selected model, fallback triggered (yes/no), latency, token count, and cost. This data feeds the ADR review cycle.
Model Router with Fallback and Circuit Breaker

# model_router.py — production-grade routing with fallback and circuit breaker
# pip install anthropic

import time
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any

import anthropic

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class CircuitState(Enum):
    CLOSED = "closed"       # Normal operation
    OPEN = "open"           # Failing — skip this model
    HALF_OPEN = "half_open" # Recovery probe allowed


@dataclass
class CircuitBreaker:
    failure_threshold: int = 5      # Open after this many consecutive failures
    recovery_timeout: float = 60.0  # Seconds before trying a probe in HALF_OPEN
    state: CircuitState = CircuitState.CLOSED
    failure_count: int = 0
    last_failure_time: float = 0.0

    def record_success(self) -> None:
        self.failure_count = 0
        self.state = CircuitState.CLOSED

    def record_failure(self) -> None:
        self.failure_count += 1
        self.last_failure_time = time.monotonic()
        if self.failure_count >= self.failure_threshold:
            self.state = CircuitState.OPEN
            logger.warning("Circuit opened after %d failures", self.failure_count)

    def is_available(self) -> bool:
        if self.state == CircuitState.CLOSED:
            return True
        if self.state == CircuitState.OPEN:
            elapsed = time.monotonic() - self.last_failure_time
            if elapsed >= self.recovery_timeout:
                self.state = CircuitState.HALF_OPEN
                logger.info("Circuit entering HALF_OPEN — sending probe")
                return True
            return False
        # HALF_OPEN: allow exactly one probe; subsequent callers are blocked
        return True


@dataclass
class ModelConfig:
    model_id: str
    cost_per_million_input_tokens: float   # USD
    cost_per_million_output_tokens: float  # USD
    median_latency_ms: float
    supports_tools: bool = True
    max_context_tokens: int = 200_000
    circuit_breaker: CircuitBreaker = field(default_factory=CircuitBreaker)


# Routing policy: ordered from highest capability to lowest cost
ROUTING_CHAIN: list[ModelConfig] = [
    ModelConfig(
        model_id="claude-opus-4-20250514",
        cost_per_million_input_tokens=15.0,
        cost_per_million_output_tokens=75.0,
        median_latency_ms=2200,
    ),
    ModelConfig(
        model_id="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        cost_per_million_input_tokens=3.0,
        cost_per_million_output_tokens=15.0,
        median_latency_ms=900,
    ),
    ModelConfig(
        model_id="claude-haiku-3-5",
        cost_per_million_input_tokens=0.8,
        cost_per_million_output_tokens=4.0,
        median_latency_ms=350,
    ),
]

# Task-type overrides: some task types skip the expensive primary model
TASK_ROUTING_OVERRIDES: dict[str, int] = {
    "classification": 2,   # Start at tertiary (haiku) — fast and cheap
    "summarisation":  1,   # Start at secondary (sonnet)
    "reasoning":      0,   # Start at primary (opus) — default
}


class ModelRouter:
    def __init__(
        self,
        client: anthropic.Anthropic,
        chain: list[ModelConfig] | None = None,
    ):
        self._client = client
        self._chain = chain or ROUTING_CHAIN

    def call(
        self,
        messages: list[dict],
        task_type: str = "reasoning",
        max_tokens: int = 1024,
        **kwargs: Any,
    ) -> tuple[anthropic.types.Message, str]:
        """
        Route a request through the model chain.

        Returns (response, model_id_used).
        Raises RuntimeError if all models are unavailable.
        """
        start_index = TASK_ROUTING_OVERRIDES.get(task_type, 0)
        tried: list[str] = []

        for config in self._chain[start_index:]:
            if not config.circuit_breaker.is_available():
                logger.info("Skipping %s — circuit OPEN", config.model_id)
                tried.append(f"{config.model_id}(circuit-open)")
                continue

            try:
                t0 = time.monotonic()
                response = self._client.messages.create(
                    model=config.model_id,
                    messages=messages,
                    max_tokens=max_tokens,
                    **kwargs,
                )
                latency_ms = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000
                config.circuit_breaker.record_success()
                logger.info(
                    "Routed to %s | task=%s | latency=%.0fms",
                    config.model_id, task_type, latency_ms,
                )
                return response, config.model_id

            except (anthropic.RateLimitError, anthropic.APIStatusError) as exc:
                config.circuit_breaker.record_failure()
                logger.warning(
                    "Model %s failed (%s) — falling back", config.model_id, exc
                )
                tried.append(config.model_id)
                continue

        raise RuntimeError(
            f"All models in fallback chain exhausted. Tried: {tried}"
        )

# Example 1: routing by task type with cost-based override
# Demonstrates that a classification task uses haiku, not opus.

import anthropic
from model_router import ModelRouter

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
router = ModelRouter(client=client)

# Simple classification — override starts at haiku (index 2)
label_response, model_used = router.call(
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Classify this support ticket: 'My invoice is wrong.'\nOutput one of: billing, technical, account",
        }
    ],
    task_type="classification",
    max_tokens=10,
)
print(f"Model used: {model_used}")          # claude-haiku-3-5
print(f"Label: {label_response.content[0].text}")

# Complex multi-step reasoning — uses opus
plan_response, model_used = router.call(
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Design a 90-day onboarding plan for a new ML engineer joining a production agentic AI team.",
        }
    ],
    task_type="reasoning",
    max_tokens=1024,
)
print(f"Model used: {model_used}")          # claude-opus-4-20250514
print(plan_response.content[0].text[:200])

# Example 2: simulate circuit breaker opening and triggering fallback
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch
import anthropic
from model_router import ModelRouter, ROUTING_CHAIN, CircuitState

client = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.Anthropic)

# Simulate primary model failing 5 consecutive times
primary_cb = ROUTING_CHAIN[0].circuit_breaker
for _ in range(5):
    primary_cb.record_failure()

print(f"Primary circuit state: {primary_cb.state}")  # CircuitState.OPEN

# Now route — primary is skipped, secondary handles the request
secondary_response = MagicMock()
secondary_response.content = [MagicMock(text="Handled by secondary")]
client.messages.create.return_value = secondary_response

router = ModelRouter(client=client, chain=ROUTING_CHAIN)
response, model_used = router.call(
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}],
    task_type="reasoning",
)

print(f"Model used despite primary failure: {model_used}")
# Output: claude-sonnet-4-5  (secondary, because primary circuit is OPEN)

# Simulate recovery: fast-forward time past recovery_timeout
ROUTING_CHAIN[0].circuit_breaker.last_failure_time -= 120  # 120 s ago
print(f"Available after timeout: {primary_cb.is_available()}")  # True (HALF_OPEN probe)

Consider a legal document analysis platform that processes hundreds of contracts per hour. Each contract passes through three pipeline stages: triage (is this a standard NDA, a complex M&A agreement, or something unusual?), clause extraction (extract all termination clauses into a structured JSON object), and risk summary (synthesise a one-page risk briefing for the reviewing attorney).
The team wrote three ADRs. ADR-014 documented that triage uses
: the benchmarks showed 97% accuracy at 12× lower cost than sonnet, with median latency of 320 ms. ADR-015 documented that clause extraction uses

: structured output reliability on complex nested JSON was significantly better than haiku, and the extra cost was justified by the downstream error rate reduction. ADR-016 documented that risk summaries use

with a fallback to sonnet: attorneys rated opus summaries as materially better on nuanced risk identification, and a quality regression to sonnet is acceptable during incidents but not as a permanent policy change.

Six months later, when claude-haiku-3-5 latency spiked due to a provider incident, the circuit breaker opened within 90 seconds and the triage stage routed to sonnet automatically. The ADR's fallback chain defined this behaviour explicitly, so the on-call engineer saw a monitoring alert saying "triage: haiku circuit OPEN, routing to sonnet — cost increase ~4x" rather than an undifferentiated error flood.

The most common mistake is writing a routing policy that optimises purely for token cost without a quality floor. A task classified as "summarisation" and routed to haiku will produce technically valid output, but if the source document is a 200-page technical specification with subtle dependencies between sections, haiku's context-window handling and reasoning depth may produce a summary that misses critical information. Always define a minimum quality threshold — measured on a held-out eval set — for each task type before choosing the cheapest model that clears it.

Fallback chains are brittle if untested. A secondary model that receives a prompt formatted for the primary model's tool-calling schema may return malformed JSON or refuse the request entirely. Teams frequently discover this only during an actual outage. Test the fallback path explicitly: write integration tests that force the primary circuit open and verify that the secondary model produces acceptable output for representative requests.
Treating the ADR as a One-Time Artifact

An ADR written at project inception and never revisited is worse than no ADR, because it gives false confidence. Model capabilities improve dramatically between generations. An ADR that says "haiku is insufficient for clause extraction" was accurate in 2023 but may no longer reflect reality. ADRs should include explicit review triggers: "re-evaluate when a new model generation is released" or "re-evaluate if extraction error rate exceeds 2%."
Write one ADR per routing dimension.

A single ADR that covers task-type routing, cost constraints, latency SLAs, and fallback chains becomes unreadable. Separate ADRs for "task-type routing policy" and "fallback and circuit-breaker policy" allow independent review.
Include benchmark data in the ADR.

An ADR that says "we chose haiku because it is cheaper" is less useful than one that says "haiku achieved 94.3% accuracy at $0.09/1k requests vs. sonnet's 96.1% at $1.12/1k requests on our test set of 500 classification tasks."
Make the router a named dependency.
Inject the router into agents and services explicitly. Never call a model directly from business logic. This ensures that the routing policy applies uniformly and can be changed in one place.
Log routing decisions in structured form.

Every request should emit a log event with: request_id, task_type, model_selected, fallback_depth (0 = primary, 1 = secondary, 2 = tertiary), latency_ms, input_tokens, output_tokens, estimated_cost_usd.
Set alert thresholds on fallback rate.
A fallback rate above 5% over a 10-minute window indicates a primary model problem that warrants investigation, not silent automatic compensation.
Define re-evaluation triggers in the ADR.
Explicit triggers — new model release, cost increase >20%, quality regression below threshold — convert the ADR from a static record into a living policy.

The routing layer itself adds negligible overhead — a few microseconds for policy evaluation — compared to the hundreds of milliseconds consumed by the LLM call. The circuit breaker state machine involves only in-memory reads and writes. Neither component is a scalability bottleneck in a single-process deployment.

In distributed deployments (multiple router instances behind a load balancer), circuit breaker state must be shared or independently maintained per instance. A per-instance circuit breaker is simpler but less accurate: one instance may open its circuit while others continue sending requests to the failing model. A shared circuit breaker (backed by Redis or a distributed cache) is more accurate but adds a network dependency to every routing decision. Most teams start with per-instance state and accept the slight inaccuracy; shared state is warranted only when per-instance thresholds diverge meaningfully.
Each model provider is a separate HTTP endpoint. Use provider-specific async clients (HTTPX with connection pooling) to avoid opening a new TCP connection on every request.
Use an async router in high-throughput services. The synchronous implementation above is easy to reason about but blocks the event loop on every LLM call. Replace
Track per-task cumulative spend. If a single agentic run exceeds a configured cost ceiling, abort and return a partial result rather than continuing to a runaway bill.

An open-source proxy that normalises the API surface across providers. Supports fallback chains, retry logic, and spend tracking out of the box. Reduces the need to build a custom router when provider-agnostic routing is the goal.
A hosted routing service that exposes dozens of models under a single API. Useful when the team wants routing-as-a-service rather than a self-managed router.
The official Python SDK includes built-in retry logic (
parameter) and exponential backoff for rate limits. This handles transient failures but not sustained outages; a circuit breaker is complementary, not redundant.
Standard observability stack. Emit routing decisions as labelled counters (model, task_type, fallback_depth) and plot fallback rates and cost per task type on a dashboard.
LLM observability platforms that capture per-request model, tokens, latency, and cost. Integrate these before building a custom logging layer.
(by Nat Pryce) is the most common CLI for creating and managing ADR files.
adds a web UI for browsing and searching ADR history.
Risk of regression on complex tasks

Routing introduces a new class of security concern: prompt leakage across model providers. When a fallback routes a request from a self-hosted or private-cloud primary to a third-party secondary, data that was intended to stay within a private environment may leave it. The fallback ADR must document the data classification of requests and assert whether each fallback target is authorised to receive that classification.

Attach a sensitivity label (public, internal, confidential, regulated) to every request. The router checks the label against each model's authorised data classes before routing. A regulated request should never fall back to a public API model.
If the task classifier is uncertain about the sensitivity level of a request, default to the most restrictive routing policy: use only models approved for the highest sensitivity class.

For compliance-regulated workloads, every fallback must be logged with request ID, original model, fallback model, reason, and timestamp. This creates the audit trail for data residency and processing location requirements.

After a fallback, run a lightweight schema check on the output before returning it to the caller. A fallback model that does not support the same tool-calling schema as the primary may return unstructured text where JSON was expected, causing silent downstream failures.
Think of model routing as a

. A shipper (your application) hands a parcel (a request) to a logistics coordinator (the router). The coordinator does not put every parcel on the same vehicle. A small letter goes by standard post. A fragile artefact goes by specialist courier. A time-critical parcel goes by same-day express. The coordinator consults a routing manifest (the ADR policy) that specifies exactly which parcels travel by which route and what the fallback is when the preferred route is disrupted. The manifest was written once, when the logistics contract was negotiated, and it captures the reasoning that a new coordinator can read on day one rather than reconstructing from scratch.

The circuit breaker is the coordinator's knowledge that a particular road is currently blocked. Rather than sending every van down a blocked road and watching them all fail, the coordinator temporarily reroutes all traffic to the alternative and sends one probe van after an hour to check whether the road has reopened. The routing ADR is the written policy that tells the coordinator which roads count as acceptable alternatives, so the decision is procedural rather than improvised.
ADR Template for Model Routing Decisions

# ADR-NNN: Model Routing Policy for <Task Type>

**Status:** Proposed | Accepted | Deprecated | Superseded by ADR-XXX
**Date:** YYYY-MM-DD
**Deciders:** [names or team]
**Context:** [1–2 sentences: what workload does this routing decision cover?]

## Decision

Route <task_type> requests to <primary_model> as the default.
Fall back to <secondary_model> on RateLimitError or APIStatusError >= 500.
Fall back to <tertiary_model> if secondary also fails.
Circuit breaker threshold: 5 consecutive failures → OPEN for 60 s.

## Rationale

| Model             | Quality (eval set) | Cost / 1k req (USD) | Median latency (ms) |
|-------------------|--------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| primary_model     | 96.2%              | $1.12               | 920                 |
| secondary_model   | 94.8%              | $0.31               | 410                 |
| tertiary_model    | 91.3%              | $0.09               | 310                 |

Minimum acceptable quality for production: 91%.
All three models clear the threshold. Primary is preferred for quality.
Secondary is the fallback because it clears the quality bar at 3.6x lower cost.
Tertiary is the emergency fallback: quality is borderline but acceptable during incidents.

## Rejected Alternatives

- **Always use tertiary:** Quality dropped below 91% on 12% of our test set.
- **Always use primary:** 12.5x higher cost than tertiary with only 4.9pp quality gain.
- **Random load-balancing:** Does not respect quality requirements; ruled out immediately.

## Consequences

Positive: 65% cost reduction vs. always-primary. SLA maintained during provider incidents.
Negative: Output quality degrades slightly during sustained fallback periods.
Monitoring: Alert if fallback_depth > 0 for >5% of requests over any 10-minute window.

## Review Triggers

Re-evaluate when:
- A new model generation is released by any provider in the chain.
- Monthly inference cost increases by >20% without volume change.
- Eval set quality for any model drops below 91%.
- Provider SLA changes for any model in the chain.

Model routing ADRs document the reasoning behind which model handles which task type — not just the outcome — so future engineers can evaluate whether the original decision still holds when the model landscape changes.

Four primary routing strategies — capability-based, cost-based, latency-based, and quality-based — are rarely used in isolation; production routers combine them as a priority-ordered policy with capability as a hard filter applied first.

Fallback chains (primary → secondary → tertiary) transform provider outages from incidents into transparent degradations. The ADR defines the chain, documents the quality delta at each level, and asserts what monitoring alert should fire when fallback depth increases.

The circuit breaker pattern prevents cascading failures by stopping traffic to a failing endpoint. Its three states — CLOSED, OPEN, HALF-OPEN — must be implemented and tested explicitly; they do not emerge from retry logic alone.

ADR templates for routing decisions should include benchmark data, rejected alternatives, monitoring thresholds, and explicit re-evaluation triggers. Without review triggers, the ADR becomes stale documentation rather than a living policy.

Data sensitivity and regulatory requirements must be encoded in the routing policy: a fallback chain that routes confidential data to a public API is a security incident waiting to happen. Classify requests at ingress and enforce data-class constraints in the router.

Log every routing decision in structured form — task type, model selected, fallback depth, latency, tokens, cost — so that the ADR's performance assumptions can be validated against real production data and updated when they drift.
